sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Termination of resuscitation (TOR) and recognition of life extinct (ROLE) require structured clinical assessment, awareness of local law and policy, and careful team decision-making.

- Do not commence or continue resuscitation if doing so is unsafe for the team.
- Do not commence resuscitation when there are unequivocal signs of death or injuries incompatible with life.
- Where available, check for a valid advance care directive, acute resuscitation plan, DNAR/NFR order, or other legally recognised direction.
- If the situation is unclear or uncertain, commence or continue resuscitation while obtaining more information.

---

## Withholding Resuscitation

**Obvious signs of death — do not commence CPR:**
- Decomposition, putrefaction, or larval infestation
- Rigor mortis or dependent post-mortem hypostasis (lividity)
- Major traumatic injuries incompatible with life (decapitation, hemicorporectomy, exsanguination)

**Expected death / Valid advance directives:**
- Valid advance care directive refusing CPR
- Family credibly reports prior expressed wishes against resuscitation
- Terminal illness where death is expected
- Voluntary Assisted Dying medication administered

**No prospect of successful resuscitation (ALL criteria required):**

*Residential Aged Care residents:*
- Age ≥80 years AND Clinical Frailty Scale score 5–9

*Community patients (unwitnessed arrest):*
- Age ≥80 years AND asystole on presentation AND unwitnessed collapse AND frailty score 5–9

*Prolonged arrest:*
- Age ≥16 years AND unwitnessed AND asystole as initial rhythm AND >10 minutes from last known signs of life to CPR commencement AND no special circumstances

---

## Ceasing Resuscitation — Minimum Times

| Clinical picture | Minimum resuscitation time |
|---|---|
| Non-shockable rhythm + EtCO₂ <25 mmHg (poor prognosis) | 30 minutes |
| Shockable rhythm OR EtCO₂ >25 mmHg OR EMS-witnessed | 45 minutes |

**Transport to ED indicated when ANY of the following apply:**
- Shockable initial or subsequent rhythm
- High or rising EtCO₂ (>25 mmHg)
- EMS-witnessed arrest
- Pre-ambulance defibrillation or ≥3 shocks delivered
- CPR-induced consciousness or spontaneous respirations
- Narrow complex PEA at rate ≥40 bpm
- Cardiac contractility seen on POCUS
- Sustained EtCO₂ >35 mmHg
- ECMO activation criteria met
- Reversible cause identified (PE, toxicology, hyperkalaemia, hypothermia)
- Paediatric patient (<16 years)
- Pregnancy

---

## CRAFTED Decision Framework

Use when considering cessation:

- **C — Cause / Special circumstances:** Cardiac origin, overdose, asphyxia, drowning, hypothermia, and pregnancy all favour prolonged resuscitation
- **R — Resuscitative Effectiveness:** Good CPR quality, CPR-induced pulses, fluctuating EtCO₂ — favour continuation
- **A — Ask and include the team:** Decision should be a team consensus, not unilateral
- **F — Futile features:** Prolonged arrest, poor EtCO₂, slow/wide PEA, comorbidities, frailty — favour cessation
- **T — Transport / Time / Handling risks:** Consider safety and feasibility of transport
- **E — Explain and engage:** Communicate empathetically with family — do NOT ask them to decide on cessation
- **D — Document:** Record full clinical findings, reasoning, consultation, and team discussion

---

## Traumatic Arrest
- Priority is damage control resuscitation and rapid transport
- Consider TOR if no ROSC after 10 minutes at scene when ED arrival cannot occur within 15 minutes of departure
- Prolonged CPR in blunt trauma without correctable cause rarely produces good outcomes

---

## Determination of Death

Document ALL of the following before certifying life extinct:
- No central pulses palpable
- Asystole for >30 seconds in ≥3 leads
- Fixed, dilated pupils with absent corneal reflexes
- No spontaneous respiratory effort
- No auscultated heart sounds

**Note:** These criteria confirm absence of activity — they are NOT prognostic for resuscitation and must NOT guide withholding/ceasing decisions.

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Residual electrical activity or pacemaker spikes may occasionally be seen after effective circulation has ceased. Do not rely on these findings alone; interpret them in the full clinical context and local ROLE or TOR criteria.
- Do not simply wait for deterioration to asystole if the overall picture remains uncertain. Reassess for reversible causes, signs of output, and the need for senior consultation.
- Explain events and decisions to family or other necessary parties with empathy, honesty, and clarity, and provide appropriate support.
- Document the time, rhythm context, consultation, and rationale for TOR or ROLE clearly.
